column40: I think the LLM was only looking at the table headers to figure out if they matched, not at what the contents were. I thought that the contents of the tables were different enough in the columns that they were about different topics and shouldn't be union'd.
column58: AI reinforced the reasoning I originally used- the columns were semantically very similar, even if the labels weren't identical.
column76: I think the AI was too hung up on the number of columns. The issue for me was the semantic inclusion of year in Table A, while Table B's 'favorability' is something that heavily depends on year, so it won't necessarily line up.
column94: Based on the previous AI explanations, it sounds like it mostly looks at structural alignment rather than semantic. I think one table was talking about the use cases/purposes of the field/operations, while the other had the class that the topic was discussed in.
column112: I agreed with the AI's reasoning, but it did not affect my reasoning.
column130: Same reasoning as the LLM; it didn't affect my reasoning, but did give me a little more confidence, though I was already highly confident.

column166: I thought that the semantic meaning of the columns were significantly different enough. Without specific AI feedback I couldn't get much useful information, but it agreeing with me did boost my confidence a bit.
column184: I'm not familiar with literary devices and content, so the AI's explanation helped to explain away my uncertainty about how unionable the tables were.
